verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But in refuting the false allegations against Macarius, he made use of legal forms; taking exception in the first place to Eusebius and his party, as his enemies, protesting against the injustice of any man’s being tried by his adversaries.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He next insisted on its being proved that his accuser Ischyras had really obtained the dignity of presbyter; for so he had been designated in the indictment.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But as the judges would not allow any of these objections, the case of Macarius was entered into, and the informers being found deficient of proofs, the hearing of the matter was postponed, until some persons should have gone into Mareotis, in order that all doubtful points might be examined on the spot.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Athanasius seeing that those very individuals were to be sent to whom he had taken exception (for the persons sent were Theognis, Maris, Theodorus, Macedonius, Valens, and Ursacius), exclaimed that ‘their procedure was both treacherous and fraudulent; for that it was unjust that the presbyter Macarius should be detained in bonds, while the accuser together with the judges who were his adversaries, were permitted to go, in order that an ex parte collection of the facts in evidence might be made.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Having made this protest before the whole Synod and Dionysius the governor of the province, and finding that no one paid any attention to his appeal, he privately withdrew.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those, therefore, who were sent to Mareotis, having made an ex parte investigation, held that what the accuser said was true.
